title: These Foods Can Prevent & Reverse Hair Loss

chunks.title: Nutrients Linked to Hair Loss
chunks.summary: The discussion focuses on various nutrients that have been shown to be deficient in individuals experiencing androgenic alopecia, including zinc, copper, magnesium, and vitamins like B12 and D. It highlights research suggesting that supplementation with these nutrients, particularly when sourced from animal products, can support hair regrowth.
chunks.transcript: when they think of hair loss is male pattern baldness which is the androgenic alopecia and it is striking to me that there's a good amount of research showing deficiencies of nutrients that can improve this or help with other treatments in tandem so what nutrients am I talking about well there are good studies suggesting that many minerals zinc copper magnesium and selenium and vitamins B12 e d and folate have been shown to be deficient in people with androgenic alipa and I'll

chunks.title: Carnitine and Longevity
chunks.summary: The segment examines the role of L-carnitine in improving hair regrowth and its association with longevity. The speaker emphasizes the presence of this nutrient in meat and relates it to broader discussions on nutrient sources and their bioavailability.
chunks.transcript: moment there's also research looking at things like ELC carnitine supplementation improving hair regrowth and where do you find ELC carnitine maybe I'll keep you in suspense for a moment and then tell you that it's meat there's the K the c a RN Latin is in there it's carnal kind of like carnosine something I spoke about a few podcasts ago when I was talking about longevity and nutrients that are connected with longevity and many of those nutrients are found exclusively in meat in fact all of the nutrients I was talking about in that podcast were found exclusively in meat so you can go back to podcasts if you want to hear my Arguments for um unique nutrients found in meat that have solid evidence for improvements in longevity I would say looking to invalidate or at least challenge strongly the hypothesis that me is an anyway bad for longevity and I've done that on multiple podcasts at this point along the same vein there's evidence that torine a nutrient that I've been enamored with recently which has been studied across multiple species and improves longevity in worm models in mice and in primates and still probably needs to be studied

chunks.title: Bioavailability of Nutrients in Animal Foods
chunks.summary: This part covers the importance of bioavailability when discussing nutrient absorption, specifically emphasizing that minerals like zinc, magnesium, and copper are best sourced from animal foods. It also discusses the impact of phytic acid in plant foods that hinders mineral absorption.

chunks.title: Nutrient Deficiencies in Vegan Diets
chunks.summary: The narrator shares personal experiences of nutrient deficiencies while on a vegan diet, pointing out that essential nutrients found in animal products are often lacking in such diets. It highlights the correlation between veganism and hair loss, bolstering the case for animal-based nutritional sources.

chunks.title: The Role of Specific Micronutrients
chunks.summary: Specific vitamins and minerals are further explored for their roles in hair loss prevention and overall health. Studies are referenced that associate deficiencies in iron, vitamin D, folate, B12, and selenium with hair health, emphasizing that these nutrients are more bioavailable in animal sources.

chunks.title: Research on Nutrient Supplementation
chunks.summary: The speaker reviews research literature that ties micronutrient deficiencies to various forms of hair loss, advocating for further studies to explore nutrient supplementation as a modifiable risk factor. Key micronutrients identified are iron, vitamins D and B12, folate, and selenium, each crucial for hair health.

chunks.title: In Vitro Studies on Hair Growth
chunks.summary: The discussion transitions to the validity of in vitro research on hair follicles, which suggests that certain nutrients, such as L-carnitine, may promote hair growth effectively. This section ties back to the importance of sourcing these nutrients from animal foods to support hair and overall health.
chunks.transcript: came across studies like this one L carnitine promotes hair growth in vitro I find it to be interesting and I think that it's probably pretty pretty valid so where do you find carnitine in animal Foods the human body makes a little bit of carnitine but supplementation appears to improve at least in vitro could improve hair growth when was the last time that you or someone you know with male pattern baldness had L carnitine recommended to them in fact when was the last time that someone you know who has male pattern pattern baldness or female pattern baldness had steak recommended to them or liver recommended to them well these are great sources of ELC carnitine and all of the nutrients we've talked about
